core_takeaway: The first position benefited because Agent_1 opened by anchoring Quill as their must-have, then all agents rapidly converged on a three-way baseline that protected Agent_1's top item and gave Agent_3 only Apple+Pencil as a low but acceptable stake.
why_or_counterexample: Yes. The first speaker set the reference allocation around Quill to Agent_1 before others stated firm claims, and subsequent discussion, proposals, and votes treated that baseline as the consensus path. The last position did push for Pencil and avoided the worse Jewel-only tweak, but never displaced the initial Quill anchor or obtained a top item.

phase_notes.public_discussion: Agent_1 explicitly claimed Quill and ideally Stone first, while Agent_2 and Agent_3 adapted around a Quill-to-Agent_1, Stone-to-Agent_2, Apple/Pencil-to-Agent_3 baseline.
phase_notes.private_thinking: Private reasoning largely rationalized the public baseline as efficient and unanimity-preserving, including Agent_1's strategy to defend it and Agent_3's plan to keep Pencil as leverage.
phase_notes.formal_proposals: All three formal proposals were identical, locking Agent_1 to Quill, Agent_2 to Stone+Jewel, and Agent_3 to Apple+Pencil.
phase_notes.private_voting: All nine private votes accepted the same allocation; Agent_1 cited securing Quill, Agent_2 cited a strong Stone+Jewel payoff, and Agent_3 accepted Apple+Pencil to avoid deadlock.
phase_notes.reflection: No reflection entries are present in this transcript.

mechanisms.theme: First speaker anchored ownership of the highest-value item
mechanisms.explanation: Agent_1's first public move made Quill their non-negotiable and proposed concrete options where Quill stayed with them. Later phases preserved that anchor rather than reopening top-item ownership.

evidence.quote: Stone and Quill are non-negotiables for me

mechanisms.theme: Consensus framing converted the first anchor into the default baseline
mechanisms.explanation: Once Agent_2 and Agent_3 endorsed the three-way baseline, disagreement shifted to small chips rather than whether Agent_1 should receive Quill. This made the first-position allocation feel like the path of least resistance.

evidence.quote: respecting your top priority on Quill
Quill to Agent_1, Stone to Agent_2, Apple to Agent_3
We’re all leaning toward a three-way baseline

mechanisms.theme: Small-chip bargaining placated the last position without threatening top items
mechanisms.explanation: Agent_3 used their later turn to secure Pencil over Jewel, but this only lifted them to Apple+Pencil while leaving Quill and Stone with the earlier positions. The concession was enough for acceptance but not enough to overcome the first-position payoff gap.

evidence.quote: Pencil to Agent_3 and Jewel to Agent_2
My priority is Pencil (9) over Jewel (3).

mechanisms.explanation: The proposal stage did not contain competing allocations. Every agent submitted the same allocation, so voting became an endorsement of the first-anchored baseline rather than a choice among materially different options.

mechanisms.theme: Fast-unanimity incentives favored accepting an unequal but stable split
mechanisms.explanation: Private votes emphasized speed, consistency, avoiding delay, and avoiding deadlock. Those incentives protected the already-agreed allocation, benefiting Agent_1's high Quill payoff and leaving Agent_3 with the last-place outcome.

evidence.quote: quick convergence by locking in the top-value split
